topic: A person discovers their teenage diary has been published online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The blue light of the laptop screen was the only illumination in the room, casting sharp shadows across Maya’s face. She tapped a frantic rhythm on the keyboard, her fingers flying across the keys as she scrolled through the comments section. The words—her words—were scattered across the internet, a digital wildfire she hadn't started but now watched burn. A cold knot tightened in her stomach, a heavy, sickening weight settling in her chest. She reread the headline, the familiar, youthful prose laid bare for the world to dissect. Her jaw clenched so tightly a muscle jumped beneath her skin. She needed to close the window, to retreat, but her eyes kept darting back, drawn to the cascade of reactions, the digital applause she hadn't asked for.

[story 2]

Liam paced the worn rug of his bedroom, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in his ears. He kept running a hand through his already dishevelled hair, his gaze flicking from the phone screen to the closed door. Every time he looked at the notification pop-up, a sharp, involuntary intake of breath hitched in his throat. He stopped pacing abruptly, his feet planting themselves firmly on the floorboards. He leaned against the wall, his shoulders slumping as a wave of intense agitation washed over him. He picked up the device, his thumb hovering over the 'share' button, a tremor running through his hand before he finally slammed the screen off, the sudden darkness feeling oppressive.

[story 3]

Sarah sat bolt upright in her chair, the cheap plastic groaning under the sudden shift in weight. Her breath came in shallow, quick gasps. She stared at the screen, the text shimmering mockingly on the display. A strange, buzzing energy vibrated just beneath her skin, making her feel physically keyed up, like a tightly wound spring threatening to snap. She pushed her chair back, the scrape of metal on wood sounding deafening in the quiet apartment. Her hands trembled slightly as she reached for the mouse, compelled by a morbid curiosity, needing to see what else the digital current had dragged her into.
